perturbation.changed_text: (n) "Territory" shall mean countries mutually agreed in writing.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of "Territory" is mainland China. Changing the definition to 'countries mutually agreed in writing' creates uncertainty, especially when schedule A has specified mainland China. There would be a contradiction with the current schedule if no country is specified or agreed in writing.
perturbation.contradicted_text: In exchange for the Share Consideration, Licensor hereby grants to Licensee a non-exclusive, royalty-free, perpetual and non-perpetual license (subject to the duration and scope, and format limitations for which Licensor has the rights to each Title as specified in Schedule A1-A6 of Schedule A) to: i. license, exhibit, distribute, reproduce, transmit, perform, display, and otherwise exploit and make available each Title within the Territory in any language by VOD (including SVOD, TVOD, AVOD and free VOD) for Internet, TV and mobile platforms (including, but not limited to, OTT streaming services, Sites and Mobile Sites), subject to these limitations for each of the Title in Schedule A1-A6:
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'Territory' as mainland China, creating a clear scope for the license granted in the contradicted text. The changed text replaces this with 'countries mutually agreed in writing,' introducing uncertainty. If no such agreement exists or if the agreed countries exclude mainland China, it directly contradicts the contradicted text, which grants a license to exploit titles 'within the Territory' (previously defined as mainland China). This contradiction makes it unclear where the license is applicable, potentially rendering the agreement unenforceable in mainland China, where it was initially intended to apply.

perturbation.original_text: Licensor will deliver each Additional Title in accordance with Section 4(a) [4) For Titles listed in Schedule A5].
perturbation.changed_text: Licensor will use commercially reasonable efforts to deliver each Additional Title.
perturbation.explanation: Section 4(a) specifies a detailed delivery process within fifteen days. Modifying Section 5 to state 'commercially reasonable efforts' introduces ambiguity and contradicts the mandatory delivery requirements outlined in Section 4(a).
perturbation.contradicted_text: (a) Within fifteen (15) days after the Effective Date or on December 31, 2015 (whichever is earlier), Licensor shall (at Licensor's sole expense), make the Titles available either online or by hard drive to Licensee or the third-party vendor specified by Licensee to provide or deliver the Titles from Licensee's or its third-party vendor's facilities. Delivery of the Titles shall be deemed complete if Licensor makes the Titles available in accordance with the previous sentence. If, from time to time, Licensee requests an alternate delivery method for the Titles and/or the implementation of Licensee's technical specifications relating to the online delivery of the Titles, then Licensor will use commercially reasonable efforts to comply with each such request.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandated delivery according to Section 4(a), which specifies a 15-day timeframe. The changed text uses 'commercially reasonable efforts,' introducing ambiguity and contradicting the fixed timeframe outlined in the contradicted text of Section 4(a). This creates uncertainty regarding the delivery timeline and weakens the obligation specified in Section 4(a), leading to a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Reports and all information contained in the Reports shall be considered Confidential Information of Licensee, and all right, title and interest in and to such Reports and information shall be owned by Licensee.
perturbation.changed_text: The Reports and all information contained in the Reports shall be jointly owned by Licensee and Licensor.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that the Reports are the Confidential Information and property of the Licensee. The modified version stipulates that the ownership of reports are jointly owned, which directly contradicts the confidentiality assertion and sole ownership established earlier.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All User information (including, without limitation, any personally identifiable information and statistical information regarding Users' use and viewing of the Titles) generated, collected or created in connection with the display of the Titles through Licensee's services shall be considered Confidential Information of Licensee, and all right, title and interest in and to such information shall be owned by Licensee.
perturbation.justification: The original text stipulates that the reports and information contained within are the sole property and confidential information of the Licensee. The changed text states that the reports are jointly owned. The contradicted text reiterates that User information is the Licensee's confidential information and property. Because of this, the change causes a contradiction with the original agreement. This is because if the reports are jointly owned, the Licensee can no longer claim sole ownership and confidentiality, contradicting the earlier statements. The introduction of joint ownership directly impacts the clauses guaranteeing Licensee's sole rights to the data, rendering those provisions unenforceable as written.
